mission.title: Install and Configure Elastic Cloud on Kubernetes (ECK)
mission.description: Elastic Cloud on Kubernetes (ECK) automates the deployment and management of the Elastic Stack on Kubernetes. It is ideal for users looking to run Elasticsearch, Kibana, and other Elastic products in a Kubernetes environment. Key trade-offs include the complexity of managing an operator and the need for Kubernetes expertise.

steps.title: Add the Elastic Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the Elastic Helm repository to your Helm client, allowing you to install ECK from the official source.

```bash

helm repo add elastic https://helm.elastic.co

helm repo update

```

> This command adds the Elastic Helm repository.
> Updates the local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs)
steps.description:
CRDs are required for the ECK operator to manage Elastic resources. This step installs them from the ECK Helm chart.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://download.elastic.co/downloads/eck/2.14.0/crds.yaml

```

> This command installs the necessary CRDs for ECK.

steps.title: Install the ECK operator
steps.description:
This step installs the ECK operator in the 'elastic-system' namespace using Helm.

```bash

helm install eck-operator elastic/eck-operator --namespace elastic-system --create-namespace --version 2.14.0

```

> Installs the ECK operator with the specified version.

steps.title: Verify the ECK operator installation
steps.description:
After installation, it's important to verify that the ECK operator is running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n elastic-system

```

> This command lists the pods in the elastic-system namespace to check the status of the ECK operator.

steps.title: Create an Elasticsearch cluster
steps.description:
This step creates an Elasticsearch cluster using a custom resource definition (CRD).

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1
kind: Elasticsearch
metadata:
  name: my-elasticsearch
  namespace: default
spec:
  version: 8.0.0
  nodeSets:
  - name: default
    count: 3
    config:
      node.store.allow_mmap: false
EOF

```

> Creates a 3-node Elasticsearch cluster.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1
kind: Elasticsearch
metadata:
  name: my-elasticsearch
  namespace: default
spec:
  version: 8.0.0
  nodeSets:
  - name: default
    count: 3
    config:
      node.store.allow_mmap: false
EOF

steps.title: Verify the Elasticsearch cluster
steps.description:
Check that the Elasticsearch cluster is up and running.

```bash

kubectl get elasticsearch -n default

```

> This command checks the status of the Elasticsearch cluster.

uninstall.title: Uninstall the ECK operator
uninstall.description:
This step removes the ECK operator and all associated resources. Be aware that this will delete all managed Elasticsearch clusters and their data.

```bash

helm uninstall eck-operator -n elastic-system

kubectl delete namespace elastic-system

```

> Uninstalls the ECK operator.
> Deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade the ECK operator
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you have a backup of your Elasticsearch data. This step upgrades the ECK operator to a newer version.

```bash

kubectl get elasticsearch -n default -o json > elasticsearch-backup.json

helm upgrade eck-operator elastic/eck-operator --namespace elastic-system --version 2.14.0

kubectl get pods -n elastic-system

```

> Backs up the current Elasticsearch configuration.
> Upgrades the ECK operator to the specified version.
> Verifies that the ECK operator pods are running after the upgrade.
commands: kubectl get elasticsearch -n default -o json > elasticsearch-backup.json

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to install CRDs'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The CRDs may not be compatible with the current Kubernetes version.

**Fix:**
Ensure you are using compatible CRDs for your Kubernetes version.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Elasticsearch cluster is not ready'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The Elasticsearch pods may not be starting due to resource constraints.

**Fix:**
Check the pod logs for errors and ensure sufficient resources are allocated.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Helm release failed'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The Helm chart version may not be compatible with the installed ECK version.

**Fix:**
Verify the Helm chart version and ensure it matches the ECK version.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Unable to connect to Elasticsearch'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** Network policies or service configurations may be blocking access.

**Fix:**
Check network policies and service configurations to ensure connectivity.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for Elasticsearch 9.x, improved TLS certificate management.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: Ensure all existing Elasticsearch clusters are upgraded to 8.x or higher before upgrading to ECK 2.14.

resolution.summary: You should see the Elasticsearch cluster status as 'Green' when running `kubectl get elasticsearch -n default`.
codeSnippets:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1
kind: Elasticsearch
metadata:
  name: my-elasticsearch
  namespace: default
spec:
  version: 8.0.0
  nodeSets:
  - name: default
    count: 3
    config:
      node.store.allow_mmap: false
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the specified resources.
